item.title: Leqembi
item.description:
• Summary: Leqembi (lecanemab), an anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody developed by Eisai and Biogen, is highlighted as a transformative therapy for Alzheimer's disease, with real-world data supporting its efficacy in slowing cognitive decline, consistent with the Clarity AD trial. However, significant challenges regarding high treatment costs and patient access barriers are noted, which could impact widespread adoption.
  • MoAs: Anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody that binds soluble amyloid-beta protofibrils to reduce amyloid plaques in the brain.
  • Sources: STAT News, STAT News, STAT News

item.title: Donanemab
item.description:
• Summary: Donanemab, an investigational anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody from Eli Lilly, faces an FDA approval delay despite positive clinical trial data from the TRAILBLAZER-ALZ studies showing significant slowing of Alzheimer's disease progression. Regulatory review continues as safety and efficacy are evaluated.
  • MoAs: Anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody that targets deposited amyloid-beta plaques to facilitate their clearance from the brain.
  • Sources: STAT News, STAT News

item.title: Neurocrine Biosciences
item.description:
• Summary: Sales of Ingrezza (valbenazine) for tardive dyskinesia remain strong, and Neurocrine Biosciences is advancing its pipeline in neurology and psychiatry, including potential new treatments for movement disorders.
  • MoAs: Valbenazine: Vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitor.
  • Sources: STAT News, STAT News

item.title: Alzheimer's Disease
item.description:
• Summary: Coverage focuses on advances in biomarker development, such as blood-based assays and amyloid PET imaging, for early detection and monitoring of Alzheimer's disease. It also explores evolving preventive strategies, with emphasis on anti-amyloid therapies like lecanemab (Leqembi) and lifestyle interventions aimed at reducing disease risk.
  • MoAs: Monoclonal antibodies targeting amyloid-beta plaques (e.g., lecanemab) to facilitate microglial-mediated clearance and slow neuronal damage, thereby delaying clinical decline in Alzheimer's disease.
  • Sources: STAT News, STAT News
